summary: I was living in the Village of Westmont, Illinois when I decided to join the Army in 1965. At the time, I was working construction and a friend and I were thinking about military service as the Vietnam War was heating up. Westmont was home for me as I prepared to take on what I thought would be a great adventure.

The Vietnam War was just starting to escalate when I joined the Army in 1965. I saw it on TV every night and believed we were stopping the Communists from taking over another country. I thought it would be like Korea, a short conflict, and I was ready to do my duty and serve my country during that time.

I enlisted in the United States Army at age 20 in 1965, motivated by a friend who was a paratrooper and the emerging reputation of the Special Forces. I was inducted in Chicago and went through basic training at Fort Knox, Kentucky. The Army was a place where all kinds of men came together, and it was about becoming a soldier regardless of background.

When I joined the Army, I wanted to become a paratrooper and then move into the United States Army Special Forces, the Green Berets, which were just becoming well known. I admired a friend who was a paratrooper and thought Special Forces would be the best. It was part of my goal to be the best in the military.

My uncle served in World War II, and my father was a railroad engineer during that time, so he was exempt from military service. While I didn't have direct family in the military except my uncle, their experiences influenced my respect for service. World War II was a backdrop to my family's history as I prepared to join the Army during Vietnam.

I was inducted into the Army in Chicago in 1965, at an induction center downtown possibly near Dearborn Street. My friends and I left Chicago by train from Union Station heading to Fort Campbell, Kentucky. Chicago was the place where my military journey officially began.

After induction, I went to Fort Knox, Kentucky for basic training in August 1965. The barracks were old World War II style with coal-fed furnaces, and the training was tough with strict discipline and daily inspections. Fort Knox was where I transformed from a civilian into a soldier, learning to live and work under Army standards.

We took a train from Chicago to Louisville, Kentucky, and then a Greyhound bus to Fort Knox, but the train actually stopped at Fort Campbell, Kentucky first. It was part of the journey to basic training, and I remember arriving in Louisville at night and seeing segregation for the first time. Fort Campbell was on the route as we moved toward Fort Knox.

When we arrived in Louisville, Kentucky by train on the way to Fort Knox, it was dark and I experienced segregation firsthand with separate water fountains and restrooms for white and black people. It was a stark reality compared to what I had seen on TV, and it stuck with me. Louisville was a significant stop on my journey from Chicago to basic training.

After arriving in Louisville, we were herded onto Greyhound buses to travel to Fort Knox for basic training. The buses were part of the Army's transportation system moving us from the train station to the training base. Greyhound Lines played a role in the transition from civilian life to military life during my induction process.
